key_points: Dorm quality varies significantly hall to hall at Missouri State
Ventilation and mattress quality are common student concerns
Hammons suites mentioned as a notable private option for freshmen
key_quotes: I have a 4 person private suite in Hammons at the moment, I was just wondering if anyone had opinions on the living situation there
How are the mattresses? Does it vary hall to hall?
I've heard ventilation can be an issue at some schools, is MO State one of them?

key_points: Making friends is a real challenge, especially for commuters and transfers
Greek life exists but is less dominant than schools like Mizzou
Introverts and non-traditional students report feeling isolated on campus
key_quotes: I commute an hour to Springfield every day, and I find it hard to be involved in student activities. Everyone seems to prefer to stay in their circle.
He didn't seem to have any friends. Tried to rush, but didn't get into a frat, isn't in any clubs.
I just transferred this year as a junior and I realized how unsatisfied I am right now, and that I need to get more connections.

key_points: Vegan mislabeling is a documented problem in dining centers
Students with dietary restrictions must go through Disability Office for accommodations
Commuter meal plan flexibility is a real concern for off-campus students
key_quotes: I saw online that there tends to be a bit of mislabeling when it comes to the vegan options at the dining centers.
After 2 calls of him not eating anything all day, I figured it out. Go to the student Disability office... the school definitely takes care of your dietary needs. A+
The idea of everything I eat coming directly out of my pocket freaks me out a bit.

key_points: Campus counseling center reviews are mixed — quality depends on who you see
Students failing semesters during family crises report navigating complex withdrawal policies
No strong Reddit presence praising mental health resources proactively
key_quotes: I had an appointment there recently and I'm curious if other people had an experience similar to mine (not great) or if it just depends on who you see.
I am currently failing/close to failing out of the Spring 2023 semester... My grandma broke her hip and passed away. I've never failed out of a semester.
I've been super overwhelmed this past semester with all in person classes.

key_points: Missouri State is consistently praised for generous merit aid to international students
Scholarship renewal terms can be confusing — students frequently ask for clarification
ProctorU fees ($100/class) added on top of tuition causing financial strain for online students
key_quotes: I got an 84k scholarship to MSU as an international student.
Online courses are forcing us to use ProctorU which is $100 per class just to take tests. I don't even know if I can afford to go anymore.
My scholarship letter says you are awarded XXX$ annually for four years. Just to confirm, that means this value will be renewed every year, right?

key_points: Some professors are late, unprepared, and visibly unknowledgeable — a recurring complaint
Lyman Briggs and Honors College are frequently cited as strong academic tracks
MSU's supply chain management program is nationally ranked #1 and widely recognized
key_quotes: It seems like especially this semester I have a slew of professors that truly don't do their jobs — 5-10 minutes late every day, absolutely unprepared.
Overall, I think MSU is a great university, but it seems like their hiring priorities have shifted.
MSU's supply chain management is #1 in the country — does MSU having the #1 SCM program even mean anything?

key_points: Withdrawal process is financially punishing — only $500 credit given after early withdrawal
One professor charged students $99 each to sign up for her activist website; students sued
Admissions office praised for personal, warm email communication with admitted students
key_quotes: They stated the school is no longer doing full refunds and will only give $500 credit to the balance left.
An MSU professor made students pay $99 each to sign up for her activist website. Now they're suing.
She said 'I remember your application well and I am thrilled to hear you have been admitted.' Out of thousands of applicants, she remembered my app.

key_points: Springfield campus is considered attractive and walkable by most students and alumni
Car vandalism in campus lots has occurred — 14 vehicles hit in one incident
USPS and FedEx reliability in Springfield is genuinely poor — packages routinely lost
key_quotes: 14 vehicles vandalized August 27 at approx 4am. Tires slashed, windows smashed, and gas caps pried off.
Packages and letters frequently never show up. Even domestic mail can take anywhere from a week to several months.
I visited on a speech and debate trip and very much want to attend when I can.

key_points: Supply chain and business programs have strong employer recognition nationally
MSU Honors College alumni have gone on to Stanford PhD programs
General MSU degree seen as solid but not prestigious outside the Midwest
key_quotes: I know someone who went to MSU for undergrad, Stanford for a Ph.D. It's not uncommon.
The name 'Michigan State' doesn't generate 'oohs' and 'ahhs' like some schools, and people on the other side of the planet haven't heard of MSU.
Consulting companies recruit UMich engineers. For MSU, if you want to just work in industry doing engineering, you'll be fine.

key_points: Exceptional value for merit scholarship recipients, especially international students
Full ride to Honors College frequently beats paying for marginally better schools
Without significant aid, costs are hard to justify compared to lower-ranked in-state alternatives
key_quotes: A full ride to the honors program at MSU is the better option in my opinion over paying $98k for UMich.
MSU gave me a ton of merit and financial aid too as an OOS applicant. It was the only OOS school I applied to since I love it so much.
I committed to MSU because it was cheap. I got into UW-Madison, U-Maryland, UCSB, UIUC.

key_points: Strong school spirit and Spartan identity — students commit with genuine enthusiasm
Large campus means you find your niche, but it takes real effort to do so
Frequently a landing spot for students who wanted elsewhere — but many end up loving it
key_quotes: You know, I went to MSU for grad school and although it's a very big school, once you find your niche, by the end it felt very cozy.
I, an international student, FINALLY have a place to go next year.
MSU Class of '27! Proud to be a Spartan!

key_points: **Administration:** Withdrawal triggers large bills — $1,300 charged for vacant roommate space
**Safety:** 14 cars vandalized on campus in a single night; university slow to publicly respond
**Faculty:** Students report suing a professor who charged $99 per student for personal activist site
key_quotes: MSU came at me with a bill of $1300 because of the vacant space. I have felt like nothing but a number at this huge university.
I saw video of it on the MSU snap story — it's super upsetting but I haven't heard the university address it.
An MSU professor made students pay $99 each to sign up for her activist website. Now they're suing.

key_points: Honors College professorial assistantship scholarship is automatic and underrated
Collaborative Care Clinic offers free healthcare services to students
Admissions staff are unusually personal and remembered individual applications
key_quotes: The professorial assistantship scholarship everyone automatically gets in the honors college is so nice.
Missouri State opens free Collaborative Care Clinic.
Out of thousands of applicants, she was able to remember my application — 'I am certain you will do great things.'
